input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A laboratory‑scale gantry crane consisting of a motorized crane frame and an electromechanical controller that manipulates a payload within a defined workspace. Its physically‑measured geometry, link lengths, and load‑handling capabilities lend themselves to detailed kinematic modelling and real‑time data logging, making it an ideal platform for demonstrating digital‑twin concepts in a controlled research setting.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The twin provides several services: (i) optimal motion‑planning and control of the gantry based on its kinematic model, (ii) continuous validation by comparing real‑time measured states against model predictions, (iii) historical trajectory and state logging for post‑hoc analysis, (iv) real‑time data visualisation via graphical interfaces, and (v) diagnostic monitoring of the physical controller. These services target researchers and educators who experiment with control algorithms, validate models, and visualise system behaviour.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is implemented with an open‑source software stack: Python 3.9 with the gantrylib package for kinematic modelling; TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) for high‑frequency telemetry storage; Eclipse Mosquitto as the MQTT broker for inter‑component messaging; Grafana for interactive visualization dashboards; and Autodesk Fusion 360 for generating the CAD models that feed the kinematic representation. All components are freely available, ensuring reproducibility and ease of deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin system follows a C4 container architectural model and is deployed in six Docker containers to separate concerns and simplify orchestration. The containers are: (1) an MQTT broker that serves as the central signaling medium for inter-service communication; (2) a twin core container housing the simulation engine and state model that represents the physical gantry crane; (3) a data store container (e.g., InfluxDB) that persists time‑series sensor and telemetry data; (4) a gateway service container responsible for ingesting sensor data from the physical crane’s onboard Raspberry‑Pi; (5) a web dashboard container that visualizes the twin’s state and analytics for operators; and (6) a monitoring/alerts container that performs anomaly detection and generates notifications. The containers communicate through MQTT topics and shared database access, creating a decoupled yet coordinated runtime environment that facilitates maintenance, scaling, and clear separation of duties within the twin system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The composite digital twin of the lab‑scale gantry crane relies on a dense data‑storage backbone implemented with a TimescaleDB time‑series database, which hosts the complete sensor and actuator state history.  The twin’s computational layer is expressed in Python, where a dedicated “Logging Application” continuously writes the real‑time crane state (position, velocity, motor currents, encoder angles) into the TimescaleDB tables.  Geometric knowledge of the crane structure is encoded in 2‑D CAD files (exported as open‑source DXF/BIM) that provide the scaffolding for the kinematic model.  The kinematic model, expressed in MATLAB/Simulink, computes the forward kinematics of cart and hoist movements from stepper motor step counts, and then feeds these expected states to a simulation module that generates full trajectory predictions.  Data and models are tightly coupled: sensor streams are not emitted directly on MQTT; rather, the Logging Application retrieves raw measurements from the Arduino controller, then writes them to the database, and uses MQTT only for inter‑service signalling between the logging service, the simulation engine, and the Continuous Validation Service.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The actuation hardware comprises two stepper motors: one drives the horizontal cart along the gantry rail, the other drives the vertical hoist that lifts and lowers small pucks.  Each motor is interfaced via a dedicated controller (e.g., A4988/DRV8825) which accepts GPIO PWM and direction signals from a Python program running on a Raspberry‑Pi.  The Python program, referred to as the “Physical Crane Interface,” submits motion commands to the controllers and reads back encoder values.  Actuation commands are latitude‑bound by the motor step limits (full step range ±90° for the hoist, ±180° along the rail), and safety limits are enforced by hard‑wired limit switches that trigger an immediate stop.  The crane also features an electromagnet attached to the hoist, powered by a 24 V DC supply, which is toggled by the interface to pick‑and‑place pucks.  All actuation is performed locally, with no direct MQTT channel for motor control; MQTT is strictly used for signalling between the Physical Crane Interface, logging, and higher‑level services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack consists of a Swing‑Angle Encoder (rotary encoder, 10 kHz sampling, ±0.1° resolution) mounted on the hoist shaft that reports the swing dynamics; an anemometer measuring ambient wind speed (0–50 m/s, 2 Hz update rate) embedded at the upper gantry section; and an Arduino microcontroller that collects these raw signals and exposes them via a serial UART to the Raspberry‑Pi.  The Pi then forwards the data to the Logging Application for persistence.  While MQTT is available as a broker for service signalling, the sensors do not publish directly to MQTT topics; instead, the Pi bundles the samples into messages that are handled internally by the logging pipeline.  Typical sampling frequencies are 100 Hz for the encoder, 5 Hz for the anemometer, and the aggregate telemetry is recorded at 10 Hz in the TimescaleDB.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service continuously compares the measured execution of trajectories against the simulated trajectories produced by the dynamic model. Discrepancies beyond configured tolerances are flagged for further inspection, ensuring that the Digital Twin maintains fidelity to real‑world behaviour.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical data from the crane is captured by the Python‑based Physical Crane Interface, which supplies the current state (e.g., position, velocity, load) to the Logging Application. The Logging Application continually writes these measurements to a local TimescaleDB database. The Continuous Validation Service later retrieves the stored data to compare executed trajectories against simulated ones. The documents do not specify serialization formats, transmission frequencies, event triggers, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms beyond this continuous logging into the database.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Control Application (using trajectory data from the Trajectory Generator Service) are sent to the Physical Crane via the Physical Crane Interface, which forwards the instructions to the crane’s hardware controllers. The documents provide no detail on validation, authentication, feedback, conflict resolution, or command execution confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All inter‑component communication occurs over a local area network. Core services (Control Application, Logging Application, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service) exchange messages via a lightweight MQTT broker; each service subscribes to the appropriate topics and publishes its status or telemetry. The Physical Crane Interface communicates with the crane controller hardware via a native TCP socket. No explicit bandwidth or latency metrics are specified in the document, and the document does not describe redundancy or security mechanisms beyond the MQTT broker’s presence.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin components are intended for on‑premise deployment on the host machine that directly controls the gantry crane. The Python containers run as local processes, utilizing a single TimescaleDB instance for persistent time‑series data. No cloud or edge deployment options are described, and the deployment model is a monolithic, single‑host architecture with no explicit scaling provisions beyond the modular container design outlined in the container diagram.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar provides runtime visualization, simulation and prediction of indoor air quality to support decision making for virus spread prevention. Details on analytics pipelines, confidence metrics or user interaction methods are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The exemplars cite an AutomationML based digital twin model and the use of Microsoft Azure cloud services, indicating alignment with industry standards for digital twin representation and cloud deployment. No explicit certification, compliance frameworks or interoperability standards are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, its controller, and its purpose for digital twin research. It correctly identifies the system as a 'lab-scale gantry crane' and mentions 'kinematic modelling' and 'data logging'. However, phrases like 'physically-measured geometry, link lengths' are slightly generic interpretations of the 'kinematic model' mentioned in the text rather than explicit details found in the source. Supported by quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane', 'The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller', 'kinematic model of the crane'.

The extraction is excellent and highly specific, accurately listing all key services mentioned in the source documents, including optimal trajectory generation, continuous validation, logging, and visualization. It correctly identifies the target users as researchers and educators. Supported by quotes: 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state', 'help researchers and educators'.

The description correctly lists the software stack (gantrylib, TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, Grafana, Fusion 360) and their roles. However, it incorrectly specifies 'Python 3.9'; the source text mentions 'Python module' and 'Python application' but does not state the version number. Supported by quotes: 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database... based on PostgreSQL', 'Eclipse Mosquitto was used', 'Grafana', 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of TimescaleDB, the 'Logging Application' in Python, and the general architecture involving CAD and kinematic models. However, it includes specific technical details that are not supported by the source text, such as claiming the kinematic model is 'expressed in MATLAB/Simulink' and that CAD files are 'exported as open-source DXF/BIM'. The source text mentions 'Autodesk Fusion360' for CAD and Python-based services, but does not mention MATLAB/Simulink or specific export formats. These unsupported claims significantly reduce the quality.

The extraction correctly identifies the two stepper motors (cart and hoist), the electromagnet, and the 'Physical Crane Interface'. However, it hallucinates specific hardware details: it lists 'A4988/DRV8825' controllers, whereas the source text explicitly identifies them as 'TMC4671-Eval' in Figure 4. It also incorrectly places the software on a 'Raspberry-Pi' when the text specifies a 'Personal Computer' (Section III.B) and invents specific step limits (±90°, ±180°) and voltage (24 V) that are not found in the document.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Swing-Angle Encoder', 'Anemometer', and the 'Arduino' microcontroller. However, it fills the description with detailed specifications that are completely absent from the source text, such as '10kHz sampling', '±0.1° resolution', '0–50 m/s' wind speed range, and '100Hz/5Hz/10Hz' sampling frequencies. It also incorrectly specifies a 'serial UART' connection to a 'Raspberry-Pi', while the source text only mentions an Arduino and a Personal Computer.

The extraction accurately describes the Continuous Validation Service, citing its function of comparing measured trajectories against simulated ones, the calculation of distance metrics, and the use of thresholds/acceptable tolerances. This aligns perfectly with the source text describing the service. Quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories.', 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold.'

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data flow from the physical crane through the interface to the database and validation service, correctly identifying the components involved. It accurately notes the absence of specific details like serialization formats. Quotes: 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations'.

The extraction correctly identifies the Control Application and Physical Crane Interface as the components responsible for sending commands. It correctly notes the lack of detail regarding validation or feedback mechanisms. The link between the Trajectory Generator and Control Application is a logical inference supported by the system's description. Quotes: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane', 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane'.

The extracted description accurately reflects the network infrastructure and communication protocols described in the source. It mentions local area network communication, MQTT broker for inter-service signaling, and the Physical Crane Interface communicating via a native TCP socket to hardware. The text explicitly lists the services (Control Application, Logging Application, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service) and the MQTT Broker container. It also notes that the Physical Crane Interface is a Python program connecting to controllers. The extraction correctly identifies the absence of bandwidth, latency, redundancy, and security details. Quotes: 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.', 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane.', 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.'

The extraction correctly describes the deployment model as on-premise, on a single personal computer (host machine), which controls the crane. It notes the use of Docker containers for specific services (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard) and Python modules running as local processes, which matches the source. The absence of cloud or edge deployment options and the monolithic, single-host architecture are supported by the text's description of the implementation and Figure 5. The mention of a single TimescaleDB instance is also accurate. Quotes: 'run all of the software on that computer.', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib.', 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.', 'A docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down with a single command.'

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses
